instruction: How do I implement a custom memory pool allocator in C++ that works with STL containers and provides O(1) allocation for fixed-size objects?
output:
<think>
A memory pool (slab allocator) pre-allocates a large block of memory and carves it into fixed-size chunks. Each allocation returns one chunk in O(1), each deallocation returns it to a free list in O(1). This eliminates malloc overhead and fragmentation for same-sized objects. To work with STL containers, it must satisfy the Allocator requirements (C++11 onwards): value_type typedef, allocate(n), deallocate(p, n), and be equality-comparable. The pool maintains a free list as an intrusive linked list — each free chunk stores a pointer to the next free chunk in its own memory (since it's not in use). When the pool is exhausted, we can either fail or grow by allocating another block. For STL compatibility, we need rebind support (container might allocate nodes of a different size than T). The modern approach is to use pmr::memory_resource as the backing, which separates allocation strategy from container type.
</think>

Fixed-size memory pool with STL allocator interface:

```cpp
#include <cstddef>
#include <memory>
#include <vector>
#include <list>
#include <cassert>

// Core pool: manages fixed-size blocks from pre-allocated memory
class MemoryPool {
public:
    explicit MemoryPool(size_t block_size, size_t block_count)
        : block_size_(std::max(block_size, sizeof(void*)))  // Min size for free list ptr
        , block_count_(block_count)
        , free_list_(nullptr)
    {
        // Allocate one contiguous chunk
        pool_ = static_cast<char*>(::operator new(block_size_ * block_count_));

        // Build free list through the blocks
        for (size_t i = 0; i < block_count_; ++i) {
            char* block = pool_ + i * block_size_;
            *reinterpret_cast<void**>(block) = free_list_;
            free_list_ = block;
        }
    }

    ~MemoryPool() {
        ::operator delete(pool_);
    }

    // Non-copyable, non-movable
    MemoryPool(const MemoryPool&) = delete;
    MemoryPool& operator=(const MemoryPool&) = delete;

    void* allocate() {
        if (!free_list_) {
            throw std::bad_alloc();
        }
        void* block = free_list_;
        free_list_ = *reinterpret_cast<void**>(block);
        return block;
    }

    void deallocate(void* ptr) {
        // Push back onto free list
        *reinterpret_cast<void**>(ptr) = free_list_;
        free_list_ = ptr;
    }

    bool owns(void* ptr) const {
        char* p = static_cast<char*>(ptr);
        return p >= pool_ && p < pool_ + block_size_ * block_count_;
    }

private:
    char* pool_;
    size_t block_size_;
    size_t block_count_;
    void* free_list_;
};

// STL-compatible allocator that uses MemoryPool
template<typename T>
class PoolAllocator {
public:
    using value_type = T;

    explicit PoolAllocator(MemoryPool& pool) noexcept : pool_(&pool) {}

    template<typename U>
    PoolAllocator(const PoolAllocator<U>& other) noexcept : pool_(other.pool_) {}

    T* allocate(size_t n) {
        if (n != 1) {
            // Pool only handles single objects; fall back for bulk
            return static_cast<T*>(::operator new(n * sizeof(T)));
        }
        return static_cast<T*>(pool_->allocate());
    }

    void deallocate(T* ptr, size_t n) {
        if (n != 1 || !pool_->owns(ptr)) {
            ::operator delete(ptr);
            return;
        }
        pool_->deallocate(ptr);
    }

    template<typename U>
    bool operator==(const PoolAllocator<U>& other) const {
        return pool_ == other.pool_;
    }

    template<typename U>
    bool operator!=(const PoolAllocator<U>& other) const {
        return pool_ != other.pool_;
    }

private:
    MemoryPool* pool_;

    template<typename U>
    friend class PoolAllocator;
};

// Usage with STL containers
int main() {
    // Pool for list nodes (~sizeof(list node) = sizeof(T) + 2 pointers)
    MemoryPool pool(sizeof(int) + 2 * sizeof(void*) + 16, 1000);
    PoolAllocator<int> alloc(pool);

    std::list<int, PoolAllocator<int>> mylist(alloc);
    for (int i = 0; i < 500; ++i) {
        mylist.push_back(i);  // O(1) allocation from pool
    }
    // All allocations from the pre-allocated pool — zero malloc calls
}
```

instruction: Show me how to implement a stack allocator (arena/linear allocator) in C++ that provides ultra-fast allocations by simply bumping a pointer, with support for LIFO deallocation.
output:
<think>
A stack allocator (also called linear allocator or bump allocator) is the simplest and fastest allocator possible — allocation is just incrementing a pointer. It's ideal for per-frame game allocations, temporary buffers, or any situation where you allocate a bunch of objects and free them all at once. The design: pre-allocate a buffer, maintain a pointer to the current position, allocate by bumping the pointer (with alignment), and support LIFO deallocation (reset to a saved marker) or full reset. No individual free — you either pop back to a marker or reset everything. Must handle alignment properly: round up the current position to the required alignment before allocating. I'll implement with alignment support, markers for LIFO, and a scoped guard that auto-resets.
</think>

Stack/arena allocator with LIFO deallocation:

```cpp
#include <cstddef>
#include <cstdint>
#include <cassert>
#include <new>
#include <type_traits>

class StackAllocator {
public:
    using Marker = size_t;  // Save point for LIFO deallocation

    explicit StackAllocator(size_t capacity)
        : buffer_(static_cast<char*>(::operator new(capacity)))
        , capacity_(capacity)
        , offset_(0)
    {}

    ~StackAllocator() {
        ::operator delete(buffer_);
    }

    StackAllocator(const StackAllocator&) = delete;
    StackAllocator& operator=(const StackAllocator&) = delete;

    // Allocate with alignment
    void* allocate(size_t size, size_t alignment = alignof(std::max_align_t)) {
        size_t aligned_offset = align_up(offset_, alignment);
        if (aligned_offset + size > capacity_) {
            throw std::bad_alloc();
        }
        void* ptr = buffer_ + aligned_offset;
        offset_ = aligned_offset + size;
        return ptr;
    }

    // Type-safe allocation
    template<typename T, typename... Args>
    T* create(Args&&... args) {
        void* mem = allocate(sizeof(T), alignof(T));
        return new (mem) T(std::forward<Args>(args)...);
    }

    // Allocate array
    template<typename T>
    T* create_array(size_t count) {
        void* mem = allocate(sizeof(T) * count, alignof(T));
        T* arr = static_cast<T*>(mem);
        for (size_t i = 0; i < count; ++i) {
            new (arr + i) T();
        }
        return arr;
    }

    // Save current position
    Marker mark() const { return offset_; }

    // Reset to saved position (LIFO deallocation)
    // WARNING: does NOT call destructors
    void reset_to(Marker marker) {
        assert(marker <= offset_);
        offset_ = marker;
    }

    // Reset everything
    void reset() { offset_ = 0; }

    size_t used() const { return offset_; }
    size_t remaining() const { return capacity_ - offset_; }

private:
    static size_t align_up(size_t offset, size_t alignment) {
        return (offset + alignment - 1) & ~(alignment - 1);
    }

    char* buffer_;
    size_t capacity_;
    size_t offset_;
};

// RAII scope guard that resets on destruction
class StackScope {
public:
    explicit StackScope(StackAllocator& alloc)
        : alloc_(alloc), marker_(alloc.mark()) {}

    ~StackScope() { alloc_.reset_to(marker_); }

    StackScope(const StackScope&) = delete;
    StackScope& operator=(const StackScope&) = delete;

private:
    StackAllocator& alloc_;
    StackAllocator::Marker marker_;
};

// Usage: per-frame game allocations
class GameFrame {
    StackAllocator frame_alloc_{1024 * 1024};  // 1MB per frame

public:
    void update() {
        frame_alloc_.reset();  // Free everything from last frame

        // All allocations this frame are ~free (just bump a pointer)
        auto* positions = frame_alloc_.create_array<float>(1000);
        auto* velocities = frame_alloc_.create_array<float>(1000);

        // Nested scope for temporary calculations
        {
            StackScope scope(frame_alloc_);
            auto* temp = frame_alloc_.create_array<float>(500);
            // temp automatically freed when scope ends
            compute_physics(positions, velocities, temp, 1000);
        }
        // temp memory reclaimed, positions/velocities still valid

        render(positions, 1000);
    }

    void compute_physics(float* pos, float* vel, float* tmp, size_t n) {}
    void render(float* pos, size_t n) {}
};

// Double-ended stack allocator (allocate from both ends)
class DoubleStackAllocator {
public:
    explicit DoubleStackAllocator(size_t capacity)
        : buffer_(static_cast<char*>(::operator new(capacity)))
        , capacity_(capacity)
        , front_(0)
        , back_(capacity)
    {}

    ~DoubleStackAllocator() { ::operator delete(buffer_); }

    void* allocate_front(size_t size, size_t align = alignof(std::max_align_t)) {
        size_t aligned = (front_ + align - 1) & ~(align - 1);
        if (aligned + size > back_) throw std::bad_alloc();
        void* ptr = buffer_ + aligned;
        front_ = aligned + size;
        return ptr;
    }

    void* allocate_back(size_t size, size_t align = alignof(std::max_align_t)) {
        size_t new_back = (back_ - size) & ~(align - 1);
        if (new_back < front_) throw std::bad_alloc();
        back_ = new_back;
        return buffer_ + back_;
    }

    void reset_front() { front_ = 0; }
    void reset_back() { back_ = capacity_; }

private:
    char* buffer_;
    size_t capacity_;
    size_t front_, back_;
};
```

instruction: How do I use C++17's std::pmr (polymorphic memory resources) to create allocator-aware containers that can switch allocation strategies at runtime?
output:
<think>
std::pmr (polymorphic memory resource) is C++17's solution to the allocator model's biggest problem: allocators are part of the container's type, so you can't mix containers with different allocators. pmr uses type erasure — all pmr containers use pmr::polymorphic_allocator<T>, and the actual allocation strategy is a runtime polymorphic object (memory_resource). You can swap strategies without changing container types. Built-in resources: new_delete_resource (normal new/delete), null_memory_resource (always throws), monotonic_buffer_resource (arena), synchronized_pool_resource (thread-safe pool), unsynchronized_pool_resource (single-thread pool). You can also write custom memory_resources. The key pattern: create a memory_resource, pass it to pmr containers, and all allocations go through that resource. Containers are now interchangeable regardless of backing allocator.
</think>

Polymorphic allocators with std::pmr:

```cpp
#include <memory_resource>
#include <vector>
#include <string>
#include <list>
#include <map>
#include <array>
#include <iostream>

// All pmr containers share the same type regardless of allocator
using PmrString = std::pmr::string;
using PmrVector = std::pmr::vector<int>;

// === Using built-in memory resources ===
void builtin_resources() {
    // 1. Stack-based monotonic buffer (no heap at all!)
    std::array<std::byte, 4096> buffer;
    std::pmr::monotonic_buffer_resource arena(
        buffer.data(), buffer.size(),
        std::pmr::null_memory_resource()  // Fail if buffer exhausted
    );

    // All these vectors use the stack buffer
    std::pmr::vector<int> nums(&arena);
    std::pmr::vector<double> floats(&arena);
    std::pmr::string name("hello from the stack", &arena);

    for (int i = 0; i < 100; ++i) {
        nums.push_back(i);  // Allocated from stack buffer
    }

    // 2. Pool resource for varied-size allocations
    std::pmr::synchronized_pool_resource pool;
    std::pmr::map<PmrString, PmrVector> registry(&pool);
    registry[PmrString("users", &pool)].push_back(42);
}

// === Custom memory resource ===
class LoggingResource : public std::pmr::memory_resource {
public:
    explicit LoggingResource(
        std::string name,
        std::pmr::memory_resource* upstream = std::pmr::get_default_resource()
    )
        : name_(std::move(name)), upstream_(upstream)
        , total_allocated_(0), total_deallocated_(0), peak_(0)
    {}

    ~LoggingResource() {
        std::cout << "[" << name_ << "] peak=" << peak_
                  << " leaked=" << (total_allocated_ - total_deallocated_)
                  << " bytes\n";
    }

protected:
    void* do_allocate(size_t bytes, size_t alignment) override {
        void* ptr = upstream_->allocate(bytes, alignment);
        total_allocated_ += bytes;
        size_t current = total_allocated_ - total_deallocated_;
        if (current > peak_) peak_ = current;
        return ptr;
    }

    void do_deallocate(void* ptr, size_t bytes, size_t alignment) override {
        upstream_->deallocate(ptr, bytes, alignment);
        total_deallocated_ += bytes;
    }

    bool do_is_equal(const memory_resource& other) const noexcept override {
        return this == &other;
    }

private:
    std::string name_;
    std::pmr::memory_resource* upstream_;
    size_t total_allocated_;
    size_t total_deallocated_;
    size_t peak_;
};

// === Allocator-aware class ===
class Document {
public:
    using allocator_type = std::pmr::polymorphic_allocator<>;

    explicit Document(allocator_type alloc = {})
        : title_(alloc)
        , sections_(alloc)
    {}

    Document(std::string_view title, allocator_type alloc = {})
        : title_(title, alloc)
        , sections_(alloc)
    {}

    void add_section(std::string_view text) {
        sections_.emplace_back(text, sections_.get_allocator());
    }

private:
    std::pmr::string title_;
    std::pmr::vector<std::pmr::string> sections_;
};

// === Runtime strategy switching ===
void process_documents(std::pmr::memory_resource* mr) {
    // Same function works with ANY memory resource
    std::pmr::vector<Document> docs(mr);
    docs.emplace_back("Doc 1", mr);
    docs.back().add_section("Introduction");
    docs.back().add_section("Chapter 1");
}

int main() {
    // Strategy 1: Logging + default heap
    LoggingResource logged("heap");
    process_documents(&logged);

    // Strategy 2: Monotonic arena (fast, no individual free)
    char buf[65536];
    std::pmr::monotonic_buffer_resource arena(buf, sizeof(buf));
    LoggingResource logged_arena("arena", &arena);
    process_documents(&logged_arena);

    // Strategy 3: Pool (good for mixed sizes)
    std::pmr::unsynchronized_pool_resource pool;
    process_documents(&pool);

    // Same function, three completely different allocation strategies!
}
```

instruction: Show me how to implement a free-list allocator in C++ that handles variable-size allocations with coalescing of adjacent free blocks to prevent fragmentation.
output:
<think>
A free-list allocator manages variable-size allocations from a pre-allocated buffer. Unlike a pool allocator (fixed-size), it handles arbitrary sizes. Each free block has a header with size and next pointer. On allocation: walk the free list to find a block >= requested size (first-fit, best-fit, or worst-fit), split if too large, return the data portion. On deallocation: add the block back to the free list and coalesce with adjacent free blocks to prevent fragmentation. Coalescing is the hard part: you need to find neighboring free blocks. The simplest approach is to keep the free list sorted by address, then on deallocation insert at the right position and merge with prev/next if contiguous. Each block (allocated or free) has a header with at least the size. Free blocks additionally have a next pointer stored in the data area. I need to handle alignment and the minimum block size (must fit a next pointer).
</think>

Free-list allocator with coalescing:

```cpp
#include <cstddef>
#include <cstdint>
#include <cassert>
#include <algorithm>
#include <new>

class FreeListAllocator {
public:
    explicit FreeListAllocator(size_t capacity)
        : buffer_(static_cast<char*>(::operator new(capacity)))
        , capacity_(capacity)
        , used_(0)
    {
        // Entire buffer is one free block
        free_list_ = reinterpret_cast<FreeBlock*>(buffer_);
        free_list_->size = capacity - sizeof(Header);
        free_list_->next = nullptr;
    }

    ~FreeListAllocator() {
        ::operator delete(buffer_);
    }

    FreeListAllocator(const FreeListAllocator&) = delete;
    FreeListAllocator& operator=(const FreeListAllocator&) = delete;

    void* allocate(size_t size, size_t alignment = alignof(std::max_align_t)) {
        // Minimum allocation size must fit a FreeBlock's data
        size = std::max(size, sizeof(FreeBlock*));

        FreeBlock* prev = nullptr;
        FreeBlock* curr = free_list_;

        // First-fit search
        while (curr) {
            // Calculate aligned data start
            uintptr_t data_start = reinterpret_cast<uintptr_t>(curr) + sizeof(Header);
            uintptr_t aligned = (data_start + alignment - 1) & ~(alignment - 1);
            size_t padding = aligned - data_start;
            size_t total_needed = padding + size;

            if (curr->size >= total_needed) {
                // Found a fit — try to split
                size_t remaining = curr->size - total_needed;

                if (remaining > sizeof(FreeBlock) + 16) {
                    // Split: create new free block after our allocation
                    FreeBlock* new_free = reinterpret_cast<FreeBlock*>(
                        reinterpret_cast<char*>(curr) + sizeof(Header) + total_needed
                    );
                    new_free->size = remaining - sizeof(Header);
                    new_free->next = curr->next;

                    // Update this block's size
                    curr->size = total_needed;

                    // Update free list
                    if (prev) prev->next = new_free;
                    else free_list_ = new_free;
                } else {
                    // Don't split — give the whole block (internal fragmentation)
                    if (prev) prev->next = curr->next;
                    else free_list_ = curr->next;
                }

                used_ += curr->size + sizeof(Header);
                return reinterpret_cast<void*>(aligned);
            }

            prev = curr;
            curr = curr->next;
        }

        throw std::bad_alloc();
    }

    void deallocate(void* ptr) {
        if (!ptr) return;

        // Walk back to find the header
        Header* header = reinterpret_cast<Header*>(
            static_cast<char*>(ptr) - sizeof(Header)
        );

        used_ -= header->size + sizeof(Header);

        // Create free block at this location
        FreeBlock* freed = reinterpret_cast<FreeBlock*>(header);

        // Insert into free list in address order (required for coalescing)
        FreeBlock* prev = nullptr;
        FreeBlock* curr = free_list_;

        while (curr && curr < freed) {
            prev = curr;
            curr = curr->next;
        }

        // Insert between prev and curr
        freed->next = curr;
        if (prev) prev->next = freed;
        else free_list_ = freed;

        // Coalesce with next block if adjacent
        if (curr && adjacent(freed, curr)) {
            freed->size += sizeof(Header) + curr->size;
            freed->next = curr->next;
        }

        // Coalesce with previous block if adjacent
        if (prev && adjacent(prev, freed)) {
            prev->size += sizeof(Header) + freed->size;
            prev->next = freed->next;
        }
    }

    size_t used() const { return used_; }
    size_t capacity() const { return capacity_; }

private:
    struct Header {
        size_t size;  // Size of data (not including header)
    };

    struct FreeBlock : Header {
        FreeBlock* next;
    };

    bool adjacent(FreeBlock* a, FreeBlock* b) const {
        return reinterpret_cast<char*>(a) + sizeof(Header) + a->size
            == reinterpret_cast<char*>(b);
    }

    char* buffer_;
    size_t capacity_;
    size_t used_;
    FreeBlock* free_list_;
};

// Usage
int main() {
    FreeListAllocator alloc(1024 * 1024);  // 1MB

    void* a = alloc.allocate(100);
    void* b = alloc.allocate(200);
    void* c = alloc.allocate(300);

    alloc.deallocate(b);  // Free middle block
    alloc.deallocate(a);  // Free first — coalesces with b's space

    // Now we have a large free block where a+b were
    void* d = alloc.allocate(280);  // Fits in coalesced space

    alloc.deallocate(c);
    alloc.deallocate(d);
}
```
